speaker: interviewer
text: This is Roll 4.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Otwock, the nurse, Helinka, the gentle nurse, Helinka, with whom I was friendly and we shared the same room, she decided to go back to Stryj. She went to Stryj. To Stryj, she had-- Stryj was more east of our town, Lesko. It was-- so it was not destroyed and she was able to go by train. She went there. She came back. She found everybody intact and alive. And she was telling me how her mother did and her, her father did. And her sister had a niece, a little baby, and she brought the pictures. Only her husband, who was in 1939 in the Polish army, he was probably taken prisoner. She didn't know by the Germans or by the Russians, if they killed him at Katyn. At that time we didn't even know about Katyn. But anyhow, her husband was not. Otherwise-- so she came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: After she returned and I heard that Lesko is already also 'free' in quotation marks, I did-- I ask would they give me now a furlough to go to see if somebody is alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, let me mention about our hospital. Our hospital had four or five Jewish doctors. They were all educated in Poland, in Lvov, in Krakow. They were Jews through and through. Even though we did not go to exclaim we are Jewish, but we didn't hide it. Everybody knew. My name was Wielopolski. Maybe some patients didn't know that I was Jewish because I did not make an issue out of it. Helinka knew that I was Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: So of course, I was given at that time a furlough to go home. And one doctor, Dr. Blum, who was from, from Boryslav in Drohobycz. This was also more east from our town. He said, when you-- when you go there, when you come to Lublin, you come there and ask about my sister because somebody told me that she survived somewhere in Plaszow, in Krakow. At that time, Krakow was still-- no. But that she survived. Not in Krakow. I'm sorry. Krakow was still German.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, I don't remember. The trains were really not running again. But as a soldier, a little bit hitchhiking, I was not aware that I could have been raped, that I could have been killed either because of the depression of knowing already what happened-- my brother was not with me. I was from [INAUDIBLE] taken to the Polish Army. He was from [INAUDIBLE] taken to a working establishment somewhere also in Russia. And from there, he was mobilized. I had no idea where my brother was. So I was really alone there. I was depressed. At that time-- or maybe one is young and very naive. I did not really realize in what-- how I endangered myself. But anyhow, I came by train to Lublin. And in Lublin was the day when Majdanek was nearby Lublin. And at that time, the Jewish survivors who survived from the area, surrounding area, were all in, in one huge buildings. I didn't go anywhere else to look. And there was a group of people who already managed to survive to do a little bit of black marketeering and exchanging a piece of bread for a piece of clothing or whatever. And at that time in Majdanek, the one who was-- the German or the Polish who was the head in Majdanek of the camp was at that time shot or hung by-- I don't know if by survivors or by the Russian or the Polish who came there. This was the time. Anyhow, I came there. I was very depressed with what I saw. And I did meet the Dr. Blum's sister. She survived the war. And she told me that nobody else of his family survived. She is the only one. She was a pharmacist and somehow she was managing either-- you know, they use the people who were intelligent and young who were giving some kind of, of service. [SIGHS] From Lublin, Lublin at that time had already the Polish temporary government. One doctor that was in our hospital, he was already elder man. And he was in, in mostly in therapeutic, like colds or pneumonia or something like that. So he gave up his, his position, and he was on a position. They made a health department in Lublin in that temporary government. I went there. And I told him that I came here, and I would long to have another few days of furlough to go to my town to Lesko. So they told me, trains, there are no trains. But you have to go first to Przemysl, and from Przemysl, you might be able to go south. There are no trains. Our town anyhow didn't have a train even before the war.  Our station was about four kilometers on the outskirt of town. Anyhow, I came to Przemysl. I came as I came. I hitchhike. There was a train and when the train stopped, I had to wait for another train, but I came to Przemysl. The first time-- the first stop was in a Jewish committee. Somehow we Jews, wherever we are in a need, we organize. And there is always something that the Jewish committee at least immediately took the name of the few survivors. They provided one with a little bit soup. They managed to, to establish some kind of nucleus. When I came to the Jewish committee, I met Meyer Finver. And there was another-- there were two or three Jewish fellows. Meyer Finver was born in our town, and he married to Przemysl. And of course, Finvers were quite a prominent family in our town. When I came there, I registered. And he told me in Lesko, there are about 10 people who survived. One from Zaslaw, two girls that were hidden because Gentiles, young fellows for some reason had pity on them or had-- or liked them, and, and two boys. But he said, in Lesko, you have absolutely nobody. Lesko is in a boundary in woods. There are still some Germans in the woods. There are still some Ukrainian nationalists, Bandera officers, which were always big anti-Semites. And there are some Polish army units which are [INAUDIBLE] officers, which are also anti-Semites. If you go, you endanger your life. And he told me that two girls who survived in the city of Sanok, they were already-- after the liberation, which was sometimes in probably in July or beginning of August, from our town in Sanok. They were killed already after the liberation by local peasants. So I would endanger my life. And he said, you have absolutely-- makes no sense for you to go because it's nothing but an abandoned city. Only the Gentiles are there. The only thing he told me-- Irene, the one with whom I originally ran away in 1942, in June 22, 1942, that we came to Sambor, but she did not follow with the Russians deep into Russia. Irene survived. She was hidden by Dr. [? Shikevich ?] together with two other professionals, one a lawyer and one a teacher. One was my teacher in my public school. Well, but he said, even Irene is not in Lesko now. Dr. [? Shikevich ?] is in Lesko because he's a doctor. He's a regional doctor. He is always there busy. Irene is not there. She is mostly in Krakow or in Sanok.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyhow, but what he told me, he received a card from Leon Finver that Leon Finver is in the Polish army. He does not know-- the Polish army with the Russians, and the Russians sort of jurisdictional supervision formed in the Soviet Russia, that Leon is in the army. He does know exactly where.

speaker: interviewee
text: But if I want to, I can look up the number where he was writing from. I took Leo's number. I took the number from his unit. And I returned back home. And it was, I think, about one week before Christmas at home. Excuse me. I came to Otwock to our hospital. I didn't go to our town Lesko. From Przemysl, it took again a while. I came to Otwock. When I came to Otwock, it was eight days before Christmas. So I went to-- there was one, one Russian doctor who was sort of like she was the supervisor. She was the head doctor coordinating the work to all other doctors. She came from Russia, but she was Jewish. And even though she never spoke about it, but we saw-- we had the feeling that she had a feeling for all of us. And I told her, look. I didn't go to my town. And now it's before Christmas. And I saw that we-- I know that we have a lot of, of Catholic patients in our hospital. I will prepare whatever I can do to prepare to make a very merry Christmas for them-- decorate, cook, make whatever you make, the dinner. But please, for Christmas Day, for Christmas Day, I would like to have another two days of furlough to go to-- because somebody who survived the war is near Praga, which is about 20 kilometers away, or 25 approximately.

speaker: interviewee
text: How did I find where this is? Because the patients that came to us, there were always little shootings. And when they came and I was inquiring, where is this number of that unit? Where is that unit? So they said, I think it's in Rembertow near Praga, which was on our side, on the Russian side of the river Vistula. When you went over the Vistula, Warsaw was on the other side. So Christmas Day, we had another Jew which was called [? Brandt. ?] He originally lived in Praga, but apparently decide-- anyhow, I don't know if Praga was divided also. But he said, you know what? I will go too. And the hospital was good enough to give me a motor vehicle already, not a horse and buggy, but a little pickup truck and that Brandt went with me and the chauffeur went with me. And we went.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came to the unit on Christmas Day and found Leon Finver from our town. And at that time, I was there-- I don't remember if I was there overnight. Probably yes, or maybe no because then Brandt also wanted to go and look. Of course, he didn't find anybody. He was heartbroken. He was-- he was in a depressed mood. But we came back to our hospital. That pickup, the chauffeur brought us back. But at that time, Leo already said, ask for a furlough for two days. And he came with a pickup to our hospital, which gave him a chance to wash up because where he was there also, you didn't-- sometimes he had, sometimes he didn't have running water. This is when I found out about the entire Holocaust, on my trip to Lublin, on my trip to Przemysl, and when I came back this was December, Christmas time. Three days-- three weeks later was the main offensive, offensiv, offensive. We crossed the bridge on the Vistula river into Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: So let me tell you. Our hospital was a huge hospital. But because we were so close to the Vistula River, they told us, apparently in collaboration with the Russians, they say take three trucks. On one truck, take a tent. Take the basic instrument. Take some medication, Lysol, whatever alcohol, whatever is need to do primary, very primitive first, first aid.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one-- on one truck was a little kitchen with the one who was cooking and some basic-- a little bit barley, a little bit whatever. By the way, in Russia, the food was very, very meager. But when we  came already to Kiwerce at that time, we had already some meat, pork meat, in boxes in tins, which was sent from United States. We felt already an improvement and even medication. While previously our supply of medication was very meager, at that time, it was meager too. We couldn't take too much on the truck. But we had some.

speaker: interviewee
text: So January 17, 1945, in the middle of the night we crossed the Vistula River. There were three trucks from our hospital. I was there as a nurse and another one who was always like making dressings in an operating room. There were two doctors. There was the kitchen and a third department. I don't remember what the third depart-- three trucks.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we went through Warsaw, Warsaw, at least the street where we drove through, was all in flames. And then came a command. You're not stopping here. You are going. The Germans are running. Mostly they drove at night, but it didn't take too much. Poland still is not-- in a truck, it's not such a huge territory. From there they told us the next stop is going to be-- there are two towns, Torun and Bydgoszcz. I don't remember if we were first in Torun and then in Bydgosczcz or first in Bydgosczcz and then in Torun. But anyhow, it's irrelevant. When we came to one town, they said, you are not stopping here. The Germans are running. We came to the second town, you're not stopping. You go, go, go. Until we came on the German-- the old German territory, a town which was called Flatow. Now it belongs to Poland and it's called Zlotow. When we came to Flatow, the German town was dead, abandoned. If somebody was hiding in their houses, they didn't show their faces. They were afraid. There was no electricity. There was no water. If there was water, we were afraid that it's poisoned. And our hospital was only prepared for very light-- for some illnesses and for light mostly muscle or, or not, not, not injuries through the bone. We were not prepared for very difficult patients. When we came here, of course, the hospital, we couldn't go in. The buildings were not adequate.

speaker: interviewee
text: So again, there was the idea of opening a tent. And I was given again in the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], what they call [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which was the admitting office, because they were in different languages-- they were Russian and Polish. And they wanted people who are coming one after the other from the battlefield. And we were not equipped to give them help.

speaker: interviewee
text: In a huge gym where there was nothing, just four huge walls and a tent, I think not even a roof, the sick, injured very heavily people, probably without legs, they came. And all I could do, I couldn't give them a drop of water because we didn't have water.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these battle casualties?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. These were battle casualties which had-- there was a unit, a field unit, which bandaged them a little bit. And they couldn't keep it there. The casualties were very heavy, and they were coming. They were sending us to us, because at that time we were the closest to the line. We were not supposed to, but there was nobody else, so they told us go, go go. And this is-- this was the function which was given to us by the Russian military, who was nearby. At that time, a few Russian doctors who were equipped and who had the knew-how-- the know-how how to do amputations or how to give more thorough, let's say, surgical treatments, they came and helped in our town-- in the not town, in the tent, which was open, which was our surgical room. But I was predominantly in that admitting office. And over 24 hours, I didn't close my eye. The people were coming in. I had to take their papers to register them. They took their knapsack and put it under their head. If they had a coat, they covered themselves with the coat. They were sleeping on a bare floor. There was not even straw to lie down. The water, if they had in their flask, if they were lucky to bring along a little bit water-- the kitchen was supposed to provide water. We couldn't take water from the well there either. A sip of water just to wet their lips. Of course, the Germans moved again. So that huge influx, after 24 hours, it slowed down a little bit. And then I was working already on a 12-hour shift. And this was, I think, the end of February, the beginning of March. For this, they called it the distinguished unselfish service that I gave to the Polish army. During the holidays, the Easter holiday when we were already again deeper and in a quieter period, I was awarded a silver medal for bravery and for-- I have never gotten that because I ran away from the Polish army before they were handing them out. But it was published in the military paper. And Leo read the paper. And he through, through someone who again had an illness and came to our hospital congratulated me that I received a medal, a silver medal. From that town Zlotow, again we were told-- we sent these heavy-- after giving them the first essential help in the tent, they were evacuated. Already some units came over, some vehicles came over. And they were evacuated further on the Polish territory, because the Polish territory had some hospitals. If they were well equipped or they had good medication, but at least they were not on the battlefield.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we sent everybody away from this particular-- this was the worst part. There were a lot of Jewish boys who gave their heart, their lives, who gave their legs, who gave their arms. They were heavily wounded. And of course by the names you could always tell who was Jewish, even if I-- but they knew that I was Jewish too. So-- These were Jewish boys in the Polish army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Of course. From the-- probably from the Russian territory. Some were even mobilized when the Russians came on the eastern part of Poland. If some, some were in the partisans or some were hidden somewhere, they got them also and indicted them.

speaker: interviewer
text: As Leo, Leon Finver was one of these Jewish boys.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, but Leo started in Russia and I started in Russia. And these also started in Russia, and they were also-- yeah, just like Leo, except that maybe they were not in the Russian army prior to that. They were taken, like my brother. My brother was also mobilized and sent to the Polish army in a-- in the-- Second, Second Army already who was-- who was formed a little bit later. So I didn't see my brother during the war at all, and I didn't know where he was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're saying that there were many Jewish boys who served in the Polish army.

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot. A lot, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: As the Russian army as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could I come back for a few minutes to something else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your friend told you that in Lesko there were from the entire city of several thousand Jews-- 4,000 or 5,000 Jews-- there were 10 survivors.

speaker: interviewee
text: These were the ones who survived either hidden or one of them that was in Zaslaw and ran away, one that was sent to, to, to Belzec and jumped off the train, and Manaster, who survived. But you could count them on the fingers of both hands. And these few, we saw after the war. Two very nice girls, my age, were hidden by Gentiles, apparently because they liked them or perhaps they were hoping that after the war, their-- the money that they will dig out from somewhere. I don't know what ulterior motive they had. But the fact is the others who survived from our town are only the ones who were sent from our town by Stalin deep to Russia and came back in 1947 or whenever the influx was from the Russian territory back. From, from that town Zaslaw, yeah-- no, not Zaslaw, excuse me. Zaslaw, I'm mixing up. Zlotow on the German territory where our hospital was first, where that huge offensive was and so many-- so many injured. We were told again, go deeper in the road towards Berlin. At that time, on one of the roads, we were totally surrounded-- not exactly by the German Army, but by leftover units who couldn't run away fast enough. They were running so far, or maybe they couldn't run so fast. And we were going on the tracks, that we were surrounded. We were staying in woods. We didn't have enough food. We had the three trucks only. And the Germans were around. And we were told we cannot go forwards, and nobody from the back can come to us because the roads are unsafe. And we were there probably a week or more, surrounded by the Germans until they sort of like thinned out. Some of them maybe went to their own homes or they undressed. But until the road got safe and, and we continued until we came-- already in April, we came to a town which was called Biesenthal. And in my opinion, it was south of Berlin, perhaps 35, 40 kilometers south of Berlin. And there, we established a hospital also in buildings. We had an X-ray department. We had a physiotherapy department. And all these doctors, somehow nobody was killed, in spite of all the dangers that we went through. And we were there. But we were not anymore loaded with these very difficult cases, because by that time the other units came closer. The other units were more equipped. They had more experienced doctors, specialized in amputations, in head injuries.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were staying in Biesenthal. And whoever-- whichever soldier we had and all kinds of illnesses, when they got better at that time we were able to send them already back on the Polish territory to Polish hospitals by some kind of way. We always were taking the directives from the Russian army who was nearby if the terrain is safe, if they can go back. If we needed some medication, they have helped with medication, with instruments and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyhow, the most crucial, heavy, terrible experience was in the city Zaslov-- not Zaslov-- Flatow, which is called Zlotow in Polish, on the same line as Torun and Bydgosczcz. And we were going towards-- deep into it to the German territory. We were staying in Biesenthal for [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] probably two months. The war ended. At least it ended for the Russian, the German capitulated. The war still went on on the Japan floor-- Japanese floor-- Japanese front between United States. But there we knew that Berlin is already taken over by the Russian soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll stop for a moment and change tapes.
